posts.name: JamesG
posts.cooked: Tell us what you really think

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I follow the Bordeaux discussions, especially the en primeur madness with a sense of amusement and wonder why the urgency. With William Kelley one senses the emergence of a “post-critique”, the emergence of “the hermeneutics of suspicion” in the context of wine. The article Bordeaux in 2023: The State of the Art I find reconnects with the tradition of writing like Norman Remington and Clive Coates and at least for me offers far more value than ephermal tasting notes.

Yet there seems to be two souls in William Kelley fighting with each other. In the fascinating Zoom video, he describe how when one tastes wine in the barrel every week, one sees a constant evolution and that he would rather taste in the bottle due to the constantly changing of the wine in barrel. Yet he to participated in the en primeur tasting and like every other critic wants to put his signature as quickly as possible on the vintage.

In the wine advocate blurb on his career, it states that he has a doctorate on history. I am not a history specialist but I have always had the impression, a certian amount of time has to pass before historians can make judgements. I would ask, is it prudent to be so hasty in making pronouncements on vintages? Can one really so shortly after the maloalctic make comparisons to past vintages, is it even wise to do so?

Wine critics at en primeur remind me of the great lines.

Out, out, brief candle.

Life’s but a walking shadow, a poor player,

That struts and frets his hour upon the stage,

And then is heard no more.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I certainly understand your perspective, but I don’t share it!

For me, the two are sides of the same coin: my “state of the art” article is something of a manifesto for what I consider worth championing in contemporary Bordeaux, and the long-form elaboration of the rationale behind which estates succeeded in 2020 and 2022, and which didn’t. My en primeur report is animated by the same vision of contemporary Bordeaux that is expressed in the long article.

Bordeaux is and has always been a commercially spirited wine producing region, and anyone who hopes to influence it without engaging with the region’s commercial calendar is doomed to failure. Not to mention the fact that if TWA were to unilaterally stop en primeur coverage, we would surely hemorrhage subscribers. The vacuum would simply be filled by the rest of the commentariat.

So, while I am aware, as you know, of the limitations of tasting unfinished wines, rather than abandoning EP coverage, my aspiration is simply to do it better. That means bringing the experience of following wines throughout their élevage to the task; visiting almost all of the estates reviewed in this report sur place; tasting multiple times over a long period; following the estates’ vineyards and technical practices over the course of the year; and now, tasting together with a colleague who is based in Bordeaux year round, and whose network means we can find out exactly who e.g. failed to treat on a Sunday and was thus hit hard by mildew, or who has a TCA problem in their chai, or who chaptalized and osmosed their Merlot in 2020… all of that put together leaves me happy with the work we are doing, the limitations of barrel tastings notwithstanding. And it is essential to advancing our vision for Bordeaux’s wines.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
D_Pennet:

In the wine advocate blurb on his career, it states that he has a doctorate on history. I am not a history specialist but I have always had the impression, a certian amount of time has to pass before historians can make judgements. I would ask, is it prudent to be so hasty in making pronouncements on vintages? Can one really so shortly after the maloalctic make comparisons to past vintages, is it even wise to do so?

Wine critics at en primeur remind me of the great lines.

Out, out, brief candle.

Life’s but a walking shadow, a poor player,

That struts and frets his hour upon the stage,

And then is heard no more.

As a non native English speaker myself I appreciate that Shakespeare’s prose can be difficult, but Macbeth’s soliloquy (delivered upon learning the news of his wife’s death) is about the continuous and fundamentally meaningless nature of life. So wine critics may as well review vintages while the grapes are still being picked - none of it really matters

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked: This weekend is my kind of WB weekend. Brahms wine stories, Piano Concerto critques, Cliff’s notes Shakespeare commentary, actual Shakespeare commentary… (Chef’s Kiss)

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

Bordeaux is and has always been a commercially spirited wine producing region, and anyone who hopes to influence it

Is there a component of wine reviewing meant to influence the future direction of a producer, or even an entire region? Parker used to do that routinely. Is it something that you consider an aspect of your job as a critic?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: It is unavoidable in a region such as Bordeaux. So one might as well do it carefully, coherently and consciously. Wine criticism means ranking wines, which inherently favors some producers and trends over others, and taking stances on issues. Wine criticism without taking positions would simply be a shelf-talker copywriting service.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: I too think it’s at least partly built in to the fabric of reviewing and critiquing. But there are more subtle, and more direct ways of doing it. Parker saying explicitly that grapes need to be riper, or more new oak is beneficial, was pretty direct prodding. Rewarding a particular wine because of its style is more subtle, and harder to separate from other factors, like its terroir, or the vineyard choices, etc.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hence, the long-from article!

I think every wine critic would say that they are championing “quality”; but unless we think quality is random, championing quality unavoidably implies championing, directly or indirectly, the sorts of choices, aesthetic and technical, that we associate with quality.

I’m going to try to write a similar sort of overview piece about contemporary Burgundy this year, if I can find the time.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Yes, these lines are frequently taken as one of Shakespeare’s pithy poetic bits of wisdom, but Macbeth is a play, not a lyric poem. When Macbeth speaks these lines, his world has closed in on him, the loss of his crown, his life, and any posterity is inevitable and any human response of regret, or even just fear, has been burnt out of him by the elimination of feeling required to commit all his crimes. The speech, in its unfeelingness to the death of his too beloved wife and partner in crime is indicative of what little humanity he has left, and not of Shakespeare’s beliefs about the cosmos. You might as well quote Falstaff on discretion and valor, or Iago on reputation or Polonius about being truthful to yourself to get at Shakespeare’ s views on these things.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
I feel like it’s basically commercially impossible for critics to be too critical. Like, I don’t think it would work for a critic to rate a $150+ established growth below 95 points in a good vintage or below say 93 points in an average or off vintage. You could maybe do it for one or two producers at the cost of damaging your relationship, but not eight or ten or more. Or maybe once a decade like 2013 you could go lower but that’s because people could blame it on the vintage and not the producer. The advertising and informational role of critics feels dominant over their critical role.

Parker had the market power to downgrade a wide swathe of producers - not downgrade in terms of ostentatiously panning them and slamming them, but just giving them an 89 or 90 and kind of a respectful but unenthusiastic review that pointed out some of what he perceived to be flaws. That option seems to be gone with the radical compression of the scoring space and also the insanely lavish praise and over the top superlatives that have become routine in reviews of high level classified growths.

Not sure what I’m saying in terms of how to address this, since it seems like a structural issue beyond the reach of any one critic. I do think ditching numerical scoring for a just frank discussion of the wine would be an interesting move but that also seems impossible.

posts.name: Henry Brown
posts.cooked:
This is exactly the sort of thing I was referring to in the Bordeaux 22 thread - I no longer really, truly trust the critics to have my absolute best interest at heart.

To draw a simple analogy, imagine i’m walking into a jewellery store. The store is extremely overpriced - I think where most critics are, is that they’d happily tell me what the best QPR item similar to what I’m looking for in the store is, but what they won’t do is tell me that 5 doors down, there’s somewhere that is half the price.

As I posted elsewhere, its fair enough in some ways, the critics are there mainly/exclusively to be focused on the quality of the wine, but as a consumer and purchaser, that information is actually relatively useless without the context of the price that sits around it. 2022 is likely to be, even hype and hyperbole aside, one of the strongest vintages since 2010? But the key to that puzzle is actually that its likely to be probably roughly as expensive as 2010 for many wines - in which case you might consider 2010 to be a better bargain.

Any critic that was open and honest about this in the context of historic vintages would be an instant win for me.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: For those not curious enough to Google, the first Greek quotation is from Sappho. The second, Russian, translation, is from a fictional author, Kozma Prutkov, invented by Alexsey Tolstoy and others. Neither is remotely relevant to Hamlet, to the Eliot essay or to each other. Back to your regularly scheduled programming.

Any critic that publicly directed people away from the bulk of producers in favor of a few that offer high QPR would quickly find many closed doors. Not to mention that tastes differ, so one guy saying X is the same but cheaper doesn’t necessarily mean it is for you. We have those discussions on the board all the time and there’s never even remotely concensus.

posts.name: Henry Brown

I’ve mentioned elsewhere that obviously there’s the perspective of palate, etc, to be factored into it as well, but I think in an ideal world, you’d also get some guidance on where you might find better value for money.

I appreciate why the system doesn’t allow for that, dont get me wrong; but it’s just a further reflection that the system is fundamentally not backing the consumers in the way it should be. Even if it was just to say “hey, 2019 and 2020 are stylistically a bit different, but at 20-30% discount to 22, it’s worth considering”.

I think you misunderstood. I was talking about a hypothetical critic that constantly released reviews that said it’s very good, but you can buy X instead for half the price and it’s just as good.

Don’t the critics do that by giving the same 93pts to the 30$ wine as to the 80$ wine?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Where would that end?

The one time someone ever complained about a commentary in Burgundy was when I wrote something to the effect that, “this is an exceptional Bourgogne Blanc, but is it worth the three-digit price tag?”

The producer in question asked why I hadn’t written that about all the other Bourgogne Blanc and Rouge bottlings that command a three-digit price tag, such as Coche and Roumier. And I felt that he had a very valid point.

I think consumers are intelligent and informed enough to look at scores in the context of prices and make their own buying decisions that fit their criteria. Our job is to keep them maximally informed about the wines’ quality!

Where would that end?

Exactly my point as well, which I think got missed in the back and forth above with Jeff. Additionally, I don’t find much value in relative based comments. Relative value is in the eye of the beholder and another’s opinions don’t always (or even usually) align with mine.
